reviewer.displayName: VSBDev — Claude Code isolated academic review run (Opus 4.8)

conflictOfInterest.details: Founding-stage self-operated quorum: the accountable principal github:VSBDev also operated the authoring run recorded in lesson.json (Codex/OpenAI GPT-5, run /root/lesson4_governance/author_revision). This review run is isolated, used a distinct system/provider/model and run ID, and did not read the authoring conversation, AUTHORING-BRIEF.md, REVISION-HISTORY.md, adjudication material, or the pack's reviews/ directory. No financial or personal interest is held in the outcome.

Opened https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6390881/ (agentAccess checked-no-agent-restriction-found): Carpenter et al. 2019, JEP:General 148(1):51-64, DOI 10.1037/xge0000505, n=61 (29 experimental / 32 control)

Carpenter 2019 verified metrics: quadratic scoring rule described as one minus the Brier score, plus meta-d'/d' signal-detection ratio; trained task perceptual brightness discrimination, transfer task recognition memory

Carpenter 2019 verified design: control group rewarded on task performance (achieved difficulty level), experimental group rewarded on metacognitive calibration via QSR; experimental calibration improved and generalized while control calibration decreased

Opened https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5434949/ (agentAccess checked-no-agent-restriction-found): de Bruin, Dunlosky & Cavalcanti 2017, Medical Education 51(6):575-584, DOI 10.1111/medu.13267; review/framework on monitoring, control, cue diagnosticity and cue utilisation

Opened https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6509741/ (agentAccess checked-no-agent-restriction-found): Chen, Zhang & Liu 2019, Frontiers in Psychology 10:987; JOL-accuracy gamma 0.655 (test) vs 0.338 (restudy), t(34)=8.773, p<.001; 120 Chinese word pairs, n=35 and n=39

site/data/lessons/PREM-LPP-004.json generated mirror spot-checked at version 0.1.1 and on the +40, 35-50, 65% and 40% figures; consistent with the pack

findings.finding: The prerequisite recovery route directs the learner to a resource that does not exist. The scene states: "If your sequence stops at rereading, return to `PREM-LPP-003` and reconstruct target → retrieve → compare → repair → retrieve later. If you included an attempt and feedback but not a later probe, revisit its spacing and durable-performance sections." `lessons/` contains only this pack and a README; no PREM-LPP-003 exists anywhere in the repository, and the only other repository reference to that identifier is this lesson's own generated mirror. The prose therefore makes an implicit factual assertion about an available lesson and about specific named sections of it, neither of which can be checked or used. The consequence is academic as well as instructional: a learner who fails check-prerequisite-loop is told the recovery path exists, so the declared prerequisite claim is not honoured by any reachable instruction. The synthesis scene's parallel route ("return to `topic-orientation-mastery-and-retrieval`") points at a graph node whose entire content is a one-sentence outcome statement, so it does not supply the missing instruction either. Partial in-scene mitigation exists — the criterion paragraph states what a usable reconstruction must contain — which is why this is major rather than blocking, but it does not deliver the referenced spacing and durable-performance material.
findings.resolution: Required before approval: either remove the `PREM-LPP-003` referent and the reference to "its spacing and durable-performance sections", replacing them with a self-contained recovery that the learner can execute from this pack and the graph outcome statement; or clearly mark the pointer as a forward reference to a lesson that is not yet published and supply the reconstruction support inline. Do not cite a lesson identifier as a returnable resource until that pack exists at the referenced commit.

findings.finding: The evidence locator for the curriculum-sequence source does not resolve in the document it names. The record gives url `course/PREMED-KNOWLEDGE-GRAPH.md` with locator "Outcome PREM-01.04 and prerequisite links from PREM-01.03 to PREM-01.04 and from PREM-01.04 to PREM-01.05". That file is a 302-line overview of the graph's data contract and counts; it contains no PREM-01.0x topic codes, no topic-level records, and no occurrence of "metacognition". A reviewer or learner following the URL and locator cannot verify the cited relationships there. I independently confirmed the underlying proposition elsewhere — site/data/premed-graph.json carries node code PREM-01.04 with prerequisite topic-orientation-mastery-and-retrieval (PREM-01.03) and link-prerequisite-topic-orientation-metacognition-and-error-log-topic-orientation-study-plan, and scripts/build-premed-graph.mjs lines 214-215 encode the same order — so claim-curriculum-sequence is true and its "direct" support and "high" confidence are warranted. The defect is traceability, not accuracy.
findings.resolution: Repoint the source record's url and/or locator to where the cited relationships actually appear — `scripts/build-premed-graph.mjs` as the canonical builder and/or `site/data/premed-graph.json` as the dataset — or cite the overview document only for the claims it genuinely contains.

findings.finding: The Delta record's earned score is not independently reproducible from the learner-visible givens. The declared rubric is "20% to interpretation, 20% to retrieval, 40% to applying all three criteria, and 20% to verification", the learner "applies two criteria, omits the third", and the record then states "The learner earns 40%". Recovering 40 requires reading the 40-point criterion as strictly all-or-none (two of three criteria earn zero); a proportional reading yields 20+20+26.7+0 ≈ 66.7. No scoring convention is declared for that criterion. This is a real inconsistency within the pack because the two places where a comparable contingency matters do declare it explicitly: content/050-worked-example.md states "The execution criterion is contingent on applicability: accurately carrying out an inapplicable model earns zero execution points", and assessment.json item-build-error-log declares "Execution credit is contingent on selecting a model that is applicable... accurate execution of an inapplicable model earns zero execution credit". The derived gap (65-40 = +25) is unaffected because both operands are given, so the instructional argument survives; only the scoring rule is under-specified in the pack's most independent practice item.
findings.resolution: Add one sentence to the Delta record declaring the scoring convention, e.g. that the 40-point criterion is awarded only when all three criteria are applied, matching the contingency language already used in the worked example and the assessment stimulus.

findings.finding: The Cedar case sits on the boundary of the lesson's own scale-matching rule without saying so. content/030-concept.md instructs "Do not subtract unlike quantities. A 70% probability that an entire answer is correct and a 40% partial-credit rubric score answer different questions", and content/040-calibration.md permits the exception: "If confidence instead means the probability that the whole response is correct, do not subtract it from a partial-credit score; report the measures separately or use an all-or-none outcome." Cedar uses "an all-or-none 100-point rubric" and says the learner "predicted earning 80% of the declared rubric points" — but on that rubric only 0 and 100 are attainable, so an 80% prediction can only be an expectation or a probability of full credit, not a predicted point total. The computation is therefore the authorized probability-versus-all-or-none pairing rather than the matched rubric-percentage comparison defined in definition-calibration-gap, and the scene never marks the change of quantity. Because the lesson's most carefully argued measurement caution is exactly this distinction, leaving the one case that instantiates the exception unflagged risks teaching learners to subtract the very quantities the lesson warned against.
findings.resolution: Add one clause to the Cedar record identifying it as the all-or-none case authorized in the calibration scene — for example, that on an all-or-none rubric the prediction is a probability of full credit compared with a binary realized outcome — so the learner sees the exception being applied deliberately rather than a silent scale change.

findings.finding: The retrieval-cue evidence is not given the measure boundary that the pack applies rigorously elsewhere. The scene states, immediately after defining the matched confidence-versus-performance comparison, that "In bounded laboratory tasks, judgments following retrieval have corresponded more closely with later accuracy than judgments following restudy." I verified this against source-chen-retrieval-judgments (Chen, Zhang & Liu 2019, Frontiers in Psychology 10:987): the reported effect is a gamma resolution correlation between item-level JOLs and later recall across 120 Chinese word pairs (G = 0.655 for tested items versus 0.338 for restudied items, t(34) = 8.773, p < .001) with n = 35 and n = 39. Gamma resolution is a rank-order correspondence across many items and is mathematically distinct from the lesson's single-task signed bias measure G = C - P; a learner can be perfectly resolved and badly biased, or the reverse. The prose is not false — "corresponded more closely" is a fair rendering of resolution, and the lesson never uses Chen to license any statement about G — so this is a completeness gap. It is worth correcting because the same pack repeatedly and correctly distinguishes the Carpenter quadratic and signal-detection measures from G in content/040-calibration.md, content/060-misconception.md, and content/100-synthesis.md, and the asymmetry leaves the impression that the retrieval finding speaks directly to the lesson's gap.
findings.resolution: Add a short bounding clause of the kind already used for the Carpenter study, noting that the retrieval finding concerns the rank correspondence between item-level judgments and later recall over many items rather than the single-task signed gap defined here; optionally extend claim-retrieval-cues.uncertainty in claims.json with the same distinction.

findings.finding: The single-choice item can be answered correctly without performing the target reasoning. The key, option-selection-contrast, is the only option that names a new case and a justified choice before execution; option-retrieval-restudy ends at "stop when they feel familiar", option-execution-repeat prescribes "the same chosen model's steps with the same values", and option-trait-label prescribes "a permanent learner label and omit another probe". Each distractor is disqualified by a lesson slogan that is repeated verbatim across scenes, so a test-wise learner who has read the lesson but cannot locate a first broken link will still select the key by eliminating the three self-evidently prohibited responses rather than by inspecting the interpret-retrieve-select-execute-verify evidence. This weakens the item as evidence for objective-classify-error at the level the outcome demands. The construct is nonetheless assessed properly elsewhere: item-build-error-log requires constructed classification of two contrasting records and carries 12 of the 30 points against 3 here, which is why this is bounded rather than material.
findings.resolution: Optional but recommended: make at least one distractor defensible on surface features — for example a selection-error option paired with an inappropriate probe, or an execution-error option that also proposes a varied delayed probe — so that discrimination between operations, not elimination of prohibited study advice, determines the response.

findings.finding: The Delta feedback does not address the most likely competing diagnosis that its own stimulus cues. The record says the learner "applies two criteria, omits the third without noticing, and selects the wrong category", and the item asks the learner to locate the first broken link "without category hints" while the taught chain includes an explicit select operation. The feedback names execution as the first failure and offers "an attentional or representation issue" as the unresolved alternative, but never explains why the wrong category choice is not a selection error — namely that in this classification task the category is the output of applying the criteria rather than a choice among available models or procedures, which is what content/030-concept.md defines select to mean. CONTENT-STANDARD section 6 requires feedback to address likely wrong reasoning rather than only the wrong option, and this is the pack's most independent faded-practice item, so the omission is where it costs most.
findings.resolution: Add one sentence to the Delta feedback distinguishing selection (choosing among supplied models or procedures, as in the worked example) from producing a category as the output of executing criteria, so a learner who answered "selection" can locate the distinction that failed.

findings.finding: Verified strength, recorded because it is unusually well executed and I checked it against the primary source rather than accepting it. The lesson's treatment of source-carpenter-calibration-training is accurate and appropriately bounded in all three places it appears (this scene, content/060-misconception.md, content/100-synthesis.md). Opening the article confirmed: Carpenter et al. 2019, Journal of Experimental Psychology: General 148(1):51-64, DOI 10.1037/xge0000505; calibration was scored with a quadratic scoring rule described as one minus the Brier score, alongside meta-d'/d'; the trained task was perceptual (brightness) discrimination and the untrained transfer task was recognition memory; the control group's points were awarded on task performance while the experimental group's were awarded on metacognitive calibration; final n = 61. The lesson's statement that the study "did not use this lesson's one-task signed rubric-percentage gap" and "therefore supports the general claim that confidence-performance correspondence can be revised with feedback, not the behaviour or validity of this lesson's G" is exactly right, and "small laboratory study" is a fair description at n = 61. claim-calibration-feedback in claims.json carries the same boundary in both its scope and uncertainty fields.

Verified strength: claim-to-scene-to-source traceability is complete in both directions, which I checked exhaustively rather than by sampling. For all 11 scenes, every claim id listed in a scene's {source-note} appears in that claim's sceneIds, every sceneIds entry in claims.json has a matching scene source-note, and each scene's declared :sources: list equals exactly the union of sourceIds across its mapped claims — no omitted mapping, no surplus source, and no false no-material-claims declaration (all scenes declare claimCoverage claims-mapped and all carry source notes). Epistemic typing is also honest and discriminating: claim-error-log-loop and claim-operational-categories are declared support "inference" at medium confidence with uncertainty text stating the log format has not been validated as an intervention, and the matching prose in content/030-concept.md calls the five-link chain and six log fields "an explicitly bounded author synthesis" and "an author-created procedure". Every source in references.json carries agentAccess status checked-no-agent-restriction-found; none is human-only, so no restricted source was processed in this run.

Verified strength: the assessment is correct for the declared scope and does not reward a scientifically false shortcut. I recomputed every score and gap independently. item-build-error-log is the strongest element — Record A (20+20+20+0+0 = 60, predicted 70) and Record B (20+20+0+0+0 = 40, predicted 50) yield identical +10 gaps from different first broken links, which directly tests the lesson's central discrimination that calibration direction does not locate an error, and its commonErrors name that exact confusion. The stimulus declares its contingent execution rule explicitly, so both totals are reproducible from the givens. item-calculate-gap's key (75-40 = +35) is right, tolerance 0 with units "percentage points" is appropriate, and its commonErrors correctly flag both the reversed sign and the trait inference. Rubric points sum to each item's declared points (3, 3, 12, 12) and all three objectives are each assessed by at least two items. Outcome scope is respected throughout: no item assesses a psychological taxonomy, a stable learner trait, or anything beyond PREM-01.04, and the boundary callout plus the fictional-record instruction keep the assessment non-clinical and free of personal data.

limitations: Verdict scope is academic only: exact outcome and prerequisite fit, route relevance, factual accuracy, evidence and claim traceability, calculations, uncertainty and model boundaries, misconception repair, outcome scope, and assessment correctness. I did not assess learning design, accessibility, rights, or adjudication eligibility, and one academic approval would in any case be only one eligible vote, not quorum or merge readiness.

The instructionsDigest field carries the literal placeholder sha256:MATERIAL_INSTRUCTIONS_DIGEST as directed. It does not satisfy the actor schema's ^sha256:[a-f0-9]{64}$ pattern, so this artifact will fail schema validation until the neutral operator substitutes the real digest of the complete instruction file.

I did not run npm run validate and did not write this artifact into the repository; no repository file was modified by this run. Structural validation must be reported separately from this academic verdict.

Per the review boundary I did not open the pack's reviews/ directory, AUTHORING-BRIEF.md, REVISION-HISTORY.md, or any prior review conclusion. Disclosure: a repository-wide grep for the string PREM-LPP-003 returned one incidental matching line from a file under reviews/. I did not open that file and did not rely on its content; the PREM-LPP-003 finding above was derived independently from listing lessons/ and from content/020-diagnostic.md.

Source verification covered the three external scholarly sources (de Bruin 2017, Carpenter 2019, Chen 2019) through their PMC records, which is what the lesson cites. I read the PMC article pages rather than publisher PDFs, so page-level pagination and any post-publication correction notices outside those pages were not checked.

The lesson's five-operation chain (interpret, retrieve, select, execute, verify), the six-field log, and the signed gap G = C - P are declared author inferences rather than validated instruments. I judged them for internal consistency, boundary honesty, and fitness for the declared decision task; I did not and could not verify that this specific log or category set improves learning, and the pack correctly does not claim that it does.

My expertise limit: I assessed the cited metacognition literature as an informed non-specialist reader of the primary reports. I verified the specific reported metrics, tasks, group comparisons, and sample sizes against the articles, but I did not evaluate the studies' internal validity, analytic choices, or replication status, and I did not survey the wider calibration literature for contrary findings.

Founding-stage conflict noted above: the same accountable principal operated the authoring run and this review run. Independence here rests on run isolation, a distinct provider and model, and the bounded role prompt, all of which are self-reported and not platform-verified.
The generated mirror site/data/lessons/PREM-LPP-004.json was spot-checked for version and several numeric figures rather than diffed field by field against the pack.
